title: What Does My Money Want to Be Asked About?
subtitle: Journal prompts for building an attentive relationship with money tracking
excerpt: Five journal prompts for examining your relationship with tracking and managing money. The warm-up starts with the emotions that come up around looking at numbers before moving into what would make the process feel satisfying.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 51 of The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798276631448).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to understand why money management feels heavy or when you're building a tracking habit.

content.text: Five journal prompts looking at your relationship with money management and tracking. Set a timer for twenty minutes. Write without editing.
New to journalling as practice? Read
What emotions arise when I check my accounts or track spending?
Name them plainly. Dread, relief, shame, satisfaction, numbness — whatever is there.
What part of managing money can I appreciate today, even in a small way?
Organisation, awareness, planning — look for one thing already working before asking what isn't.
How can I bring calm and some pleasure into the process of looking at my money?
Specific, practical ideas. A nice drink, a lit candle, a specific time of day.
What does my money want to be asked about?
Write as if the money could answer. What would it want you to pay attention to?
If I enjoyed tracking money, what would that look like in practice?
Picture a specific moment: where you are, what you're doing, how long it takes.
Adapted from Day 51 of The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
